name: Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop
name_original: Puertos de Santa Inés y Montenegro — circular entre las Sierras de Urbión y Cebollera
description: This difficult 43.2-mile (69.6 km) road cycling loop tackles the Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes with 5479 feet (1670 metres) of climbing.
region_intro: Road cycling around Vinuesa, located in the province of Soria, Spain, offers diverse terrain for cyclists. The region is characterized by mountainous landscapes, extensive pine forests, and significant natural features such as the Picos de Urbión and the Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo. Well-paved roads traverse areas with notable elevation changes, providing both challenging climbs and scenic, undulating paths. Glacial formations, including the Laguna Negra de Urbión, are also prominent features within the cycling environment. Best road cycling routes around Vinuesa The most popular road cycling route is Montenegro, Peña Hincada, and Santa Inés Passes Loop, a 62.7 miles (100.9 km) trail that takes 5 hours 44 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through mountainous passes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Laguna Negra de Urbión — circular desde Vinuesa, a moderate 23.5 miles (37.8 km) path. This route offers views of the glacial Laguna Negra and surrounding pine forests. Local road cyclists also love the Views of the Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo — round trip from Vinuesa, a 14.4 miles (23.3 km) trail leading through landscapes around the reservoir, often completed in about 1 hour 12 minutes. Road cycling around Vinuesa is defined by mountainous passes, dense pine forests, and reservoir-side paths. The network offers options for easy rides around the Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo, as well as challenging climbs towards the Picos de Urbión and Sierra Cebollera. The routes in Vinuesa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Vinuesa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Vinuesa?
A: Vinuesa offers a diverse selection of over 20 road cycling routes. These routes traverse varied landscapes, from challenging mountain passes to serene reservoir-side paths, catering to different skill levels.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Vinuesa?
A: You can expect a mix of mountainous landscapes, extensive pine forests, and routes around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2721568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo&lt;/a&gt;. The region features well-paved roads with notable elevation changes, including climbs towards the Picos de Urbión and the Sierra Cebollera, as well as gentler, undulating paths.

Q: Are there easy or family-friendly road cycling routes in Vinuesa?
A: Yes, Vinuesa offers 3 easy road cycling routes. For a relaxed ride, consider routes around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2721568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo&lt;/a&gt;, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256255342/views-of-the-embalse-de-la-cuerda-del-pozo-round-trip-from-vinuesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo — round trip from Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt;, which is 23.3 km long and takes about 1 hour 12 minutes to complete.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Vinuesa has 11 difficult routes. Options include the demanding climb to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2516913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de Urbión&lt;/a&gt; from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070381311/laguna-negra-de-urbion-circular-desde-vinuesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Negra&lt;/a&gt;, or the extensive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256296972/montenegro-pena-hincada-and-santa-ines-passes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montenegro, Peña Hincada, and Santa Inés Passes Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 100 km with significant elevation gain.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of significant natural features. You can cycle towards the enigmatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070381311/laguna-negra-de-urbion-circular-desde-vinuesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Negra de Urbión&lt;/a&gt;, nestled between granite walls and pine forests, or explore paths around the vast &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2721568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also home to the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2516913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Picos de Urbión&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3328794" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Duero River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Vinuesa?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops. A popular moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070381311/laguna-negra-de-urbion-circular-desde-vinuesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Negra de Urbión — circular desde Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt;, a 37.8 km path offering views of the glacial lake. For a longer, more challenging loop, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256284658/puerto-de-santa-ines-y-puerto-de-piqueras-circular-por-el-parque-natural-de-sierra-cebollera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Santa Inés y Puerto de Piqueras — circular por el Parque Natural de Sierra Cebollera&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Vinuesa?
A: The road cycling routes in Vinuesa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, the well-maintained roads, and the stunning natural beauty, particularly around the Laguna Negra and the Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Vinuesa?
A: The best time for road cycling in Vinuesa is generally during the spring and autumn months when the weather is milder and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can also be pleasant, especially in the mornings or late afternoons, though some climbs can be demanding under direct sun. Winter cycling is possible but may be affected by snow and colder temperatures, particularly at higher elevations like the Picos de Urbión.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints along the routes?
A: Yes, the region offers several scenic viewpoints. The road to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1070381311/laguna-negra-de-urbion-circular-desde-vinuesa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Negra&lt;/a&gt; provides captivating views as the environment transitions from pine forests to granite formations. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5573853" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lost Love Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is a notable spot to take in the surrounding vistas.

Q: What kind of elevation gain can I expect on the routes?
A: Elevation gain varies significantly across routes. Easier routes around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2721568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Cuerda del Pozo&lt;/a&gt; might have around 270 meters of ascent. More challenging routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1256296972/montenegro-pena-hincada-and-santa-ines-passes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montenegro, Peña Hincada, and Santa Inés Passes Loop&lt;/a&gt;, can feature over 2,300 meters of elevation gain, requiring significant endurance.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks near the cycling routes?
A: While the focus is on natural beauty, you might encounter points of interest such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2721569" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Submerged Roman Bridge of Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt;, visible depending on the water levels of the reservoir. The charming village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2721568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt; itself offers a glimpse into local culture and history.
tips: Santa Inés is to the north of the province and it begins on its southern slope in the town of Vinuesa. Port of entity and that lasts several kilometers, where in the middle there is a detour to go to the Laguna Negra.
Dividing point of the autonomous communities of La Rioja and Castilla León, specifically Soria. Privileged viewpoint of the Sierra de Urbión.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, the Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop is classified as a "difficult" road cycling route, requiring very good fitness. It involves significant elevation gains and considerable length, making it unsuitable for beginners. Cyclists should be prepared for a substantial challenge.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this loop?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on paved roads, but conditions can vary. The lower parts of the road on Puerto de Montenegro, for example, can have bumpy asphalt and frequent potholes, though it improves after passing through Viniegra de Arriba. Both Santa Inés Pass and Puerto de Montenegro feature long ascents with varying gradients, including some steep stretches and challenging sections like a horseshoe bend on Santa Inés Pass with 8-9% gradients.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, the loop often starts and ends in towns like Montenegro de Cameros or nearby villages. You would typically find parking in these settlements to begin your ride. Public transport options are generally limited in this mountainous region, so driving to a starting point is common.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop is approximately 69.5 kilometers long with over 1600 meters of elevation gain. Komoot estimates the duration at around 4 hours and 9 minutes for a racebike. However, variations of this route can be longer (up to 126 km) and take 5-6 hours, depending on your fitness level and stops.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally from late spring through early autumn. Be aware that the mountain passes, particularly Puerto de Montenegro, can be closed due to snow as late as May or even in early autumn. Always check local weather and road conditions before planning your trip.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights or viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The route is renowned for its spectacular natural beauty and stunning vistas as you gain elevation. Key highlights include the challenging climbs over Santa Inés Pass and Puerto de Montenegro. The scenery often follows the Viniegra River, passing through forests and opening up to expansive views towards the summits. The area is part of the Sierra de la Demanda, known for its picturesque landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Parque Natural de la Sierra Cebollera?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural de la Sierra Cebollera. However, it's always advisable to respect local regulations, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of the natural environment. Check the official park website for any updated information or specific rules regarding certain areas.

faqs.question: Is the Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a challenging road cycling route, it is not typically considered suitable for dogs. The focus is on long distances and significant climbs on paved roads, which are not ideal for canine companions. If you are looking for dog-friendly outdoor activities in the region, consider hiking trails specifically designated for that purpose.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other notable cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop intersects with sections of several other routes. You'll find it overlaps with parts of the [CIMA SO09] Santa Inés * Montenegro de Cameros (26% of the route), [CIMA SO09] Santa Inés * Vinuesa (23%), and [CIMA SO01] Montenegro (Viniegras) (15%). Smaller sections also coincide with Etapa 02: Vinuesa – Soria and Ruta del Duero.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenges or road conditions I should be aware of on this loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, be aware that some sections of the road, particularly the lower parts of Puerto de Montenegro, can have poor asphalt with potholes. While traffic is generally light, the climbs themselves are demanding, with Santa Inés Pass featuring a particularly challenging horseshoe bend with 8-9% gradients. Also, snow can close the passes until late spring or early autumn, so always check conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or water stops along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through small settlements like Montenegro de Cameros and Viniegra de Arriba, where you might find limited amenities such as cafes or small shops. However, given the mountainous and somewhat remote nature of the area, it's highly recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks for the entire duration of your ride, as services may be infrequent.
